beginner.name: SAT-level vocabulary and comprehension
beginner.description: Advanced SAT vocabulary, rhetorical analysis, passage-based reasoning

questions.question: In the sentence 'The senator's meretricious arguments, though superficially compelling, failed to withstand rigorous scrutiny,' what does 'meretricious' mean?
options: Apparently attractive but lacking real value
questions.explanation: Meretricious means apparently attractive but having no real value or integrity. It's a false cognate with 'merit' - a common SAT trap.

questions.question: Which rhetorical strategy does the following employ? 'We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds, we shall fight in the fields and in the streets.'
questions.explanation: Anaphora is the repetition of a word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses. Churchill's speech is the canonical example.

questions.question: 'The plangent notes of the cello echoed through the cathedral.' What quality does 'plangent' describe?
questions.explanation: Plangent means having a loud, reverberating, and often melancholy sound. From Latin 'plangere' (to beat the breast in grief).

questions.question: A passage argues: 'Democracy fails because voters are rationally ignorant - the cost of becoming informed exceeds any individual's marginal impact on electoral outcomes.' This is an example of:
questions.explanation: This is a core argument from public choice theory (Downs, Caplan). Rational ignorance is the economic argument that voters lack incentive to become informed because their individual vote has negligible impact.

questions.question: 'The author's tendentious interpretation of the data, while ostensibly objective, revealed an underlying ideological commitment.' What does 'tendentious' mean?
options: Expressing a strong opinion or bias
questions.explanation: Tendentious means expressing or intending to promote a particular cause or viewpoint. Not related to 'tentative' or 'tendency' in the casual sense.

questions.question: Identify the logical structure: 'If P then Q. Not Q. Therefore not P.'
questions.explanation: Modus tollens (denying the consequent) is a valid deductive argument: If P→Q and ¬Q, then ¬P. Contrast with the fallacy of denying the antecedent (¬P therefore ¬Q).

questions.question: In literary criticism, what does 'apophatic' discourse refer to?
options: Discourse that defines something by stating what it is not
Discourse that breaks the fourth wall
questions.explanation: Apophatic (from Greek 'apophasis') means defining by negation - saying what something is NOT rather than what it is. Originally from negative theology, now used broadly in literary theory.

questions.question: 'The committee's exiguous budget precluded any meaningful research initiative.' What does 'exiguous' mean?
questions.explanation: Exiguous means very small in size or amount; scanty. From Latin 'exiguus.' A precise word for expressing inadequacy of resources.

questions.question: Which of these sentences contains a dangling modifier?
options: Running through the park, the dog chased the squirrel.
Having finished the exam, the students left the room.
Walking to school, the rain began to fall heavily.
After eating dinner, she read a book.
questions.explanation: 'Walking to school, the rain began to fall' has a dangling modifier - 'walking to school' has no logical subject (rain can't walk). The sentence implies rain was walking.

questions.question: A critic writes: 'The novel's diegetic world collapses when the narrator directly addresses the reader.' What does 'diegetic' mean in this context?
options: Related to dietary themes in the text
The internal fictional world of the narrative
questions.explanation: Diegetic refers to the internal world of the narrative - the story-world as experienced by the characters. Breaking diegesis (metalepsis) occurs when the narrator steps outside this frame.

questions.question: 'His sardonic wit, far from alienating his audience, served as a vehicle for uncomfortable truths.' 'Sardonic' most precisely means:
questions.explanation: Sardonic implies grim, mocking humor often directed at human folly. Distinguished from 'sarcastic' (which intends to wound) - sardonic humor has a darker, more resigned quality.

questions.question: What fallacy is committed here? 'Either we ban all social media or we accept the total destruction of democracy. Since banning all social media is impractical, democracy is doomed.'
questions.explanation: False dichotomy (false dilemma) presents only two options when more exist. The argument ignores regulation, reform, media literacy, and other intermediate positions.

questions.question: In semiotics, what is the relationship between 'signifier' and 'signified' according to Saussure?
questions.explanation: Saussure's fundamental insight: the relationship between signifier (sound-image) and signified (concept) is arbitrary - there's no natural connection between the word 'tree' and the concept of a tree.

questions.question: 'The politician's speech was a masterclass in paralepsis.' Paralepsis is:
options: Drawing attention to something by claiming to pass over it
Using paradox to make a point
questions.explanation: Paralepsis (also apophasis) is mentioning something by saying you won't mention it: 'I won't even bring up my opponent's scandal...' It's a powerful rhetorical device.

questions.question: What does 'palimpsest' mean when used metaphorically in literary criticism?
options: A text with multiple layers of meaning, where earlier versions show through
questions.explanation: A palimpsest literally is a manuscript page reused after scraping off earlier text. Metaphorically, it describes any text/place/thing where traces of earlier versions remain visible beneath the surface.

questions.question: 'The researcher's proleptic conclusions, stated before presenting evidence, undermined the paper's credibility.' What does 'proleptic' mean?
options: Anticipatory; occurring before the proper time
questions.explanation: Proleptic means anticipating future events or conclusions. In rhetoric, prolepsis is addressing counterarguments before they're raised. Here it means jumping to conclusions prematurely.

questions.question: Which best describes Derrida's concept of 'differance'?
options: The difference between languages in translation
The way meaning is always deferred through chains of signification
The disagreement between critics about a text's meaning
The distinction between fiction and nonfiction
questions.explanation: Differance (Derrida's neologism combining 'differ' and 'defer') argues that meaning is never present in itself but is always deferred through endless chains of signifiers referencing other signifiers.

questions.question: 'Her laconic response - a single raised eyebrow - conveyed more than any speech could.' 'Laconic' derives from:
options: Greek 'Lakon' referring to Spartans, known for brevity
questions.explanation: Laconic comes from Lakonia (Sparta). Spartans were famous for terse speech. When Philip II threatened 'If I invade Lakonia, I shall raze Sparta,' the Spartans replied with one word: 'If.'

questions.question: A passage states: 'The hegemonic discourse naturalizes contingent power relations, rendering them invisible to those within its ambit.' This perspective aligns with:
questions.explanation: This is Gramscian hegemony - the idea that dominant groups maintain power not through force alone but by making their worldview seem natural and inevitable, obscuring its constructed nature.

questions.question: What is the key distinction between 'imply' and 'infer'?
options: The speaker implies; the listener infers
'Imply' is about logic; 'infer' is about emotion
questions.explanation: Imply = to suggest indirectly (speaker's action). Infer = to deduce or conclude from evidence (listener's action). One of the most commonly confused pairs in English.

questions.question: 'The essay's peroration built to a crescendo that left the audience in stunned silence.' A 'peroration' is:
options: The concluding part of a speech, typically with emotional force
A digression from the main argument
questions.explanation: Peroration is the concluding part of a speech, designed to inspire and move. Classical rhetoric divided speeches into exordium (intro), narratio, confirmatio, refutatio, and peroratio.

questions.question: Identify the zeugma:
options: 'She broke his heart and his favorite vase.'
'The thunder roared and the lightning flashed.'
questions.explanation: Zeugma uses one word to govern two others in different senses - 'broke' applies literally to the vase but metaphorically to the heart. It creates wit through semantic incongruity.

questions.question: In Foucault's framework, what does 'episteme' refer to?
options: The underlying framework of assumptions that defines what counts as knowledge in a given era
questions.explanation: Foucault's 'episteme' is the deep structural framework governing what can be thought, said, and known in a historical period. Different epistemes make different kinds of knowledge possible.

questions.question: 'The critic accused the novel of solipsism.' In this context, solipsism most likely means:
options: Self-absorbed perspective that ignores external reality
questions.explanation: Solipsism (from Latin 'solus' alone + 'ipse' self) philosophically holds that only one's own mind is sure to exist. In literary criticism, it describes work excessively focused on the self.

questions.question: What distinguishes a 'syntagmatic' relationship from a 'paradigmatic' one in linguistics?
options: Syntagmatic is about word order; paradigmatic is about word choice
Syntagmatic is about meaning; paradigmatic is about grammar
Syntagmatic is written; paradigmatic is spoken
Syntagmatic is modern; paradigmatic is archaic
questions.explanation: Syntagmatic = the horizontal chain (how words combine in sequence: 'the cat sat'). Paradigmatic = the vertical axis of substitution (what could replace each word: the/a/my + cat/dog/bird + sat/stood/lay).

intermediate.name: GRE-level advanced vocabulary
intermediate.description: Graduate-level vocabulary, complex analogical reasoning, rhetorical analysis

questions.question: 'The author's anodyne prose, devoid of any controversial stance, exemplified the worst tendencies of institutional writing.' 'Anodyne' means:
options: Inoffensive to the point of being bland
questions.explanation: Anodyne literally means 'pain-relieving' but is used to mean blandly inoffensive, unlikely to cause disagreement or offense. The pejorative sense implies cowardly avoidance of substance.

questions.question: What is the 'hermeneutic circle' in textual interpretation?
options: The tendency for interpretations to become circular arguments
The principle that understanding a whole text requires understanding its parts, and vice versa
A group of scholars who interpret texts together
The cycle of texts being reinterpreted across generations
questions.explanation: The hermeneutic circle (Schleiermacher, Gadamer) recognizes that comprehending any text requires oscillating between parts and whole - you can't understand a sentence without the paragraph, or the paragraph without sentences.

questions.question: 'The philosopher's sesquipedalian style alienated lay readers.' 'Sesquipedalian' means:
options: Characterized by the use of long words
questions.explanation: Sesquipedalian (from Latin 'sesquipedalis' = a foot and a half long) describes the use of excessively long words. The word itself is sesquipedalian - a delicious self-referential irony.

questions.question: In narrative theory, what is 'free indirect discourse'?
options: A narrator reporting a character's thoughts in third person while adopting their voice and perspective
questions.explanation: Free indirect discourse (style indirect libre) blends narrator and character voice: 'She walked to the window. What a horrible day it was!' - whose thought is 'horrible'? This ambiguity is its power. Flaubert and Austen mastered it.

questions.question: 'The documentary's treatment of the conflict was remarkably evenhanded, though critics detected a subtle tendentiousness beneath its apparent neutrality.' The sentence's internal tension is between:
questions.explanation: The sentence itself enacts the tension it describes: 'evenhanded' vs 'tendentiousness beneath apparent neutrality.' The critics see bias hidden under objectivity - the sentence mirrors this structural irony.

questions.question: What does Barthes mean by 'the death of the author'?
options: The biographical context of an author is irrelevant to textual meaning
All authors eventually die and their works outlive them
questions.explanation: Barthes (1967) argued that a text's meaning comes from the reader, not the author's intentions. The 'Author-God' who fixes meaning is replaced by the reader as the site where meaning is produced. This was revolutionary for literary theory.

questions.question: 'Her arguments were characterized by a kind of casuistry that, while logically defensible, felt morally evasive.' 'Casuistry' means:
options: Specious or excessively subtle reasoning, especially about moral issues
questions.explanation: Casuistry originally meant case-based moral reasoning (legitimate in ethics), but acquired a pejorative sense of clever but ultimately misleading argumentation designed to justify questionable actions.

questions.question: Which best describes 'defamiliarization' (ostranenie) as theorized by Shklovsky?
options: Making foreign literature accessible to domestic audiences
Artistic technique that presents common things in unfamiliar ways to force fresh perception
The process by which avant-garde art becomes mainstream
questions.explanation: Shklovsky's ostranenie (1917) argues that art's purpose is to make the stone stony - to disrupt habitual, automated perception and force us to see the world anew. It's the foundational concept of Russian Formalism.

questions.question: 'The text's aporia - the irresolvable contradiction at its core - is precisely what makes it productive for analysis.' In deconstructive criticism, 'aporia' refers to:
options: An irresolvable internal contradiction that destabilizes the text's apparent meaning
A gap in the historical record
questions.explanation: Aporia (Greek: 'without passage') in deconstruction names the moment where a text's logic breaks down, where its claims undermine themselves. For Derrida, these are not flaws but revelations of how meaning works.

questions.question: What is the 'intentional fallacy' as defined by Wimsatt and Beardsley?
options: The error of writing with too clear an intention
The mistake of judging a work by the author's stated or presumed intention rather than the work itself
Intentionally misleading readers about a text's meaning
The fallacy of believing all writing is intentional
questions.explanation: Wimsatt and Beardsley (1946) argued that the author's intention is neither available nor desirable as a standard for judging a literary work. The text must stand on its own. This became a cornerstone of New Criticism.

questions.question: 'The interlocutor's captious objections derailed every productive line of inquiry.' 'Captious' means:
options: Tending to find fault or raise petty objections
questions.explanation: Captious means disposed to find fault, especially over trivial matters. From Latin 'captiosus' (deceptive). A captious critic nitpicks rather than engaging substantively.

questions.question: In Bakhtinian theory, 'heteroglossia' refers to:
options: The use of multiple languages in a single text
The coexistence of multiple social voices, perspectives, and registers within any utterance
questions.explanation: Bakhtin's heteroglossia describes how every utterance contains multiple competing social languages - class, profession, generation, ideology. The novel is the genre that most fully orchestrates this multiplicity.

questions.question: 'The researcher's soi-disant expertise crumbled under cross-examination.' 'Soi-disant' means:
questions.explanation: Soi-disant (French: 'self-saying') means self-styled or self-proclaimed. It always carries skepticism about the claim - calling someone a 'soi-disant expert' implies they aren't really one.

questions.question: What does Genette mean by 'focalization' in narrative theory?
options: The camera-like focus of a film adaptation
The perspective through which narrative information is filtered
The central theme of a literary work
The process of literary canon formation
questions.explanation: Genette distinguished focalization (who perceives?) from narration (who speaks?). Zero focalization = omniscient; internal = through a character; external = only surface behavior. This replaced the imprecise term 'point of view.'

questions.question: 'His magnum opus was an exercise in logomachy - brilliant word-battles that produced more heat than light.' 'Logomachy' means:
options: The study of logos and branding
A dispute about or over words
questions.explanation: Logomachy (Greek: logos + mache = word + battle) means a dispute about words rather than substance, or an argument based on semantic distinctions rather than real disagreement.

questions.question: Which concept does the 'Sapir-Whorf hypothesis' advance?
options: All languages share a universal grammar
Language structure influences thought and perception
Written language is superior to spoken language
questions.explanation: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis (linguistic relativity) proposes that the structure of a language affects the worldview and cognition of its speakers. The strong version (determinism) is largely rejected; the weak version (influence) has empirical support.

questions.question: 'The scholar's exegesis of the passage revealed layers of meaning invisible to casual readers.' 'Exegesis' is:
options: A digression from the main topic
Critical interpretation or explanation of a text, especially scripture
The study of exits and endings
questions.explanation: Exegesis (Greek: 'to lead out') is detailed, careful interpretation of a text. Originally biblical criticism, now used for any close reading. Its opposite is 'eisegesis' - reading meaning INTO a text.

questions.question: In phenomenological criticism, what does 'Lebenswelt' (lifeworld) refer to?
options: The fictional world of the text
The pre-theoretical, lived experience that grounds all knowledge
The cultural context of literary production
questions.explanation: Husserl's Lebenswelt is the pre-reflective world of immediate experience before scientific abstraction. In literary criticism, it grounds analysis in how texts emerge from and speak to lived human experience.

questions.question: 'The argument proceeded by enthymeme rather than syllogism.' An enthymeme is:
options: An emotional appeal disguised as logic
A syllogism with an unstated premise
questions.explanation: An enthymeme is a syllogism with one premise left implicit. 'She must be intelligent; she went to MIT.' The unstated premise: 'People who go to MIT are intelligent.' Aristotle considered it the most effective rhetorical device.

questions.question: What does 'catachresis' mean in rhetoric?
options: The misuse or strained use of a word beyond its proper meaning
questions.explanation: Catachresis is the forced extension of a word beyond its normal usage - like 'the leg of a table' or 'I will speak daggers.' In deconstruction, it reveals how all language is ultimately catachrestic - all meaning is metaphorical.

questions.question: 'The palilalia of the text - its compulsive repetitions - suggested an underlying anxiety the author could not directly express.' 'Palilalia' literally refers to:
options: Involuntary repetition of words or phrases
questions.explanation: Palilalia is a speech disorder involving involuntary repetition. Used metaphorically in criticism, it describes textual repetition that seems compulsive rather than deliberate, suggesting unconscious preoccupation.

questions.question: According to Gadamer, what role do 'prejudices' (Vorurteile) play in understanding?
options: They always distort understanding and must be eliminated
They are the necessary preconditions for any understanding whatsoever
They apply only to legal interpretation
They are unique to non-academic readers
questions.explanation: Gadamer rehabilitated 'prejudice' (pre-judgment) arguing that we can only understand from within a tradition. Our pre-understandings aren't obstacles but the very ground of comprehension. Understanding is always historically situated.

questions.question: 'The film's mise en abyme - a painting within the film depicting the film's own plot - created a vertiginous recursive effect.' 'Mise en abyme' is:
options: A French filmmaking technique using mirrors
A self-reflective structure where a work contains a smaller copy of itself
The arrangement of objects in a scene
The placement of the camera at a low angle
questions.explanation: Mise en abyme (placed into abyss) is a formal technique where a work contains a miniature replica of itself - like a painting within a painting, or a play within a play (Hamlet). It creates infinite regression.

questions.question: What is 'negative capability' as Keats defined it?
options: The ability to write effective villains
Being capable of being in uncertainties, mysteries, and doubts without irritably reaching after fact and reason
The power of literary works to negate political systems
A poet's ability to use negative constructions effectively
questions.explanation: Keats (1817): negative capability is when one 'is capable of being in uncertainties, mysteries, doubts, without any irritable reaching after fact and reason.' It's the capacity to dwell in ambiguity - essential to artistic perception.

questions.question: 'The text's proliferating signifiers resist any attempt at totalization.' This statement reflects:
questions.explanation: Post-structuralism (Derrida, Barthes) holds that signifiers endlessly refer to other signifiers, never arriving at a stable signified. 'Totalization' (fixing complete meaning) is impossible because meaning is always in play.

advanced.name: PhD-level academic vocabulary
advanced.description: Doctoral-level critical theory, advanced rhetoric, philosophical linguistics

questions.question: In Deleuze and Guattari's framework, what does 'deterritorialization' describe?
options: The process by which established codes, norms, or territories are disrupted and their components freed for new connections
questions.explanation: Deterritorialization describes how any assemblage (social, linguistic, psychological) has its coding disrupted, freeing components to enter new arrangements. It's paired with reterritorialization - the recapture of freed elements into new structures.

questions.question: What does Spivak mean by the question 'Can the subaltern speak?'
options: Whether illiterate people can participate in democracy
Whether marginalized groups can represent themselves within dominant discourse without having their voice appropriated or distorted
Whether translation between languages is possible
Whether children should speak in classroom settings
questions.explanation: Spivak (1988) argues the subaltern cannot 'speak' because the structures of knowledge production systematically silence them. Even well-meaning representation by intellectuals can reinscribe the violence of colonialism.

questions.question: 'The pharmakon, as Derrida reads Plato, is simultaneously remedy and poison.' This concept illustrates:
options: The undecidability that haunts binary oppositions
A translation error in Plato's texts
questions.explanation: Derrida's reading of Plato's Phaedrus shows that 'pharmakon' (writing) is both remedy (aids memory) and poison (destroys it). This undecidability cannot be resolved - it deconstructs the speech/writing hierarchy Plato tries to maintain.

questions.question: In Agamben's political philosophy, what is 'bare life' (nuda vita)?
options: Life in a state of nature before civilization
Life stripped of all political qualification, reduced to mere biological existence and thus exposed to sovereign violence
Life as depicted in naturalist literature
questions.explanation: Agamben's 'bare life' (from Homo Sacer) describes life excluded from political community yet captured by sovereign power - the figure who can be killed but not sacrificed. The concentration camp is its paradigmatic space.

questions.question: What is 'haecceity' in Duns Scotus's philosophy, as adopted by Deleuze?
options: The property of being here and now
The irreducible thisness or individuality of a particular entity
The essence shared by all members of a species
questions.explanation: Haecceity (from Latin 'haec' = this) is the unrepeatable thisness that makes something this particular thing and not another. Deleuze uses it for non-personal individuations - a season, a wind, a time of day can have haecceity.

questions.question: Jameson argues that postmodernism is 'the cultural logic of late capitalism.' By this he means:
options: Capitalism has entered its final stage
Postmodern aesthetics (pastiche, depthlessness, nostalgia) are not autonomous cultural developments but structural expressions of multinational capital's mode of production
Modern art is superior to postmodern art
Late capitalism produces logical cultural products
questions.explanation: Jameson (1991) reads postmodern culture (Warhol, Portman's Bonaventure Hotel) not as a style choice but as the necessary cultural form of a specific economic stage - late/multinational/consumer capitalism.

questions.question: What does Judith Butler mean by 'gender performativity'?
options: Gender is a theatrical performance one can freely choose
Gender identity is constituted through the repeated stylization of the body - acts, gestures, and enactments that produce the illusion of a stable gender core
Gender is performed differently in different cultures
Performing gender roles in daily life
questions.explanation: Butler (Gender Trouble, 1990) argues there is no pre-existing gender identity that is then expressed. Rather, gender is produced through the repetition of stylized acts. The 'core' is an effect, not a cause, of these performances.

questions.question: In Lacan's psychoanalysis, what is the 'Real'?
options: That which resists symbolization absolutely - the traumatic kernel that cannot be captured in language or imagery
The realistic depiction of life in literature
questions.explanation: Lacan's Real is not 'reality.' It is what remains after the Symbolic (language) and Imaginary (images) have done their work - the impossible, traumatic remainder that resists all representation. It can only be glimpsed in its effects.

questions.question: 'The supplement, as Derrida shows, both adds to and replaces that to which it is added.' This logic of supplementarity reveals:
options: That additions to texts are always improvements
That the 'original' was never complete or self-sufficient to begin with
That supplements should be removed to recover original meaning
questions.explanation: Derrida's logic of the supplement (from Of Grammatology): if something needs a supplement, it was already incomplete. Writing 'supplements' speech, masturbation 'supplements' sex - but this means the 'original' was never fully present.

questions.question: What is 'interpellation' in Althusser's theory of ideology?
options: The process by which ideology constitutes individuals as subjects by 'hailing' them into their social positions
The interpolation of texts into new contexts
questions.explanation: Althusser's interpellation: ideology calls out to us ('Hey, you there!') and in recognizing ourselves as addressed, we become subjects of that ideology. We are always-already interpellated - there is no position outside ideology.

questions.question: Benjamin's concept of 'aura' describes:
options: The glow around sacred objects in religious art
The unique presence of an artwork embedded in its tradition and history, which mechanical reproduction destroys
An artist's personal style or signature
The emotional atmosphere of a literary work
questions.explanation: Benjamin (1935) defined aura as a work's unique existence in time and space - its authenticity, its embeddedness in tradition. Photography and film destroy aura by enabling infinite reproduction. This has revolutionary political potential.

questions.question: What is 'dissemination' in Derrida's sense, as distinct from 'polysemy'?
options: The spread of ideas through publication
While polysemy implies multiple but recoverable meanings, dissemination describes an irreducible scattering of meaning that cannot be gathered back into unity
The distribution of seeds as a metaphor for writing
questions.explanation: Polysemy = a word has multiple meanings (dictionary can list them). Dissemination = meaning disperses beyond any recoverable plurality. It's not that meaning is 'many' - it's that it is structurally uncontainable.

questions.question: Heidegger's 'Dasein' is best understood as:
options: Being-there: the distinctive way humans exist, characterized by being an issue for themselves and having an understanding of Being
questions.explanation: Dasein (being-there) is Heidegger's term for human existence specifically as the entity that questions its own being. Dasein is not a 'what' (essence) but a 'who' - always already in a world, thrown into possibilities, being-toward-death.

questions.question: In Ranciere's political aesthetics, what is the 'distribution of the sensible' (partage du sensible)?
options: How art is distributed to the public
The system that determines what is visible, sayable, and thinkable in a given social order - who can speak and what counts as speech
How profits from art are shared
questions.explanation: Ranciere's 'distribution of the sensible' describes how a social order implicitly determines what can be perceived, said, and done - who counts as a political subject and whose noise counts as speech. Politics disrupts this distribution.

questions.question: What is 'jouissance' in Lacanian/Barthesian theory, and how does it differ from 'plaisir'?
options: Jouissance is simply the French word for pleasure
Plaisir is comfortable, confirming pleasure; jouissance is an excessive, destabilizing, almost painful bliss that shatters the subject
Jouissance is physical; plaisir is intellectual
They are synonyms used by different theorists
questions.explanation: Barthes (The Pleasure of the Text): plaisir comes from texts that comfort and confirm. Jouissance comes from texts that unsettle, fragment, and push beyond the subject's boundaries. Lacan: jouissance exceeds the pleasure principle into a transgressive beyond.

questions.question: 'The trace, for Derrida, is neither present nor absent.' This paradox is central to:
options: The deconstruction of the metaphysics of presence - the Western philosophical assumption that being is fundamentally self-presence
questions.explanation: The 'trace' marks the absence within presence. Every sign carries traces of what it differs from. Meaning depends on absence (what a sign is NOT). This deconstructs the Western metaphysical privileging of presence, speech, and origin.

questions.question: In Kristeva's theory, the 'semiotic chora' refers to:
options: A chorus of voices in a text
The pre-linguistic realm of drives, rhythms, and pulsions that disrupts symbolic (linguistic) order from within
A type of Greek dramatic form
The study of signs and symbols
questions.explanation: Kristeva's 'semiotic' (not Saussure's) names the pre-Oedipal realm of bodily drives and rhythms (the chora). It persists within language as rhythm, musicality, and disruption - especially in poetic language which lets the semiotic breach the symbolic.

questions.question: What does 'sous rature' (under erasure) mean in Derridean practice?
options: Writing a word, then crossing it out but leaving both visible - indicating the term is inadequate but necessary
questions.explanation: Sous rature: writing a word and crossing it through (like Heidegger's Being). The crossed-out word remains readable because it is both necessary (we have no better word) and inadequate (it carries metaphysical baggage we want to refuse).

questions.question: Adorno's claim that 'to write poetry after Auschwitz is barbaric' has been widely interpreted to mean:
options: Aesthetic representation of atrocity risks aestheticizing suffering and making it consumable, raising fundamental questions about art's relationship to historical catastrophe
Only prose can address the Holocaust
questions.explanation: Adorno (1949) raised the question of whether aesthetic form inevitably transfigures suffering into something bearable, thereby betraying it. He later revised: it may be necessary to write poetry, but art must acknowledge its own impossibility in the face of such horror.

questions.question: In de Man's deconstructive reading, 'allegory' differs from 'symbol' because:
options: Allegory acknowledges the gap between sign and meaning that symbol tries to hide behind an illusion of organic unity
Symbol is visual; allegory is verbal
Allegory is medieval; symbol is modern
questions.explanation: De Man (Allegories of Reading) argues the Romantic privileging of symbol (organic unity of form and meaning) over allegory represses the truth that all language is allegorical - meaning is always displaced, never organically present.

questions.question: What is Levinas's concept of 'the face' (le visage)?
options: The human face as depicted in portraiture
The ethical encounter with the Other's vulnerability, which precedes and grounds all knowledge - an infinite demand that cannot be reduced to comprehension
Physical appearance as a marker of identity
The public persona versus private self
questions.explanation: For Levinas, the face of the Other is not a physical feature but an ethical event. Before I can know or categorize the Other, I am already called to responsibility by their vulnerability. Ethics precedes ontology.

questions.question: Zizek's reading of ideology through Lacan argues that ideology functions most powerfully:
options: Not when people believe false ideas, but when they act AS IF they believe even while knowing better - ideology structures practice, not just belief
questions.explanation: Zizek: 'They know very well what they are doing, but they do it anyway' (cf. Sloterdijk's 'cynical reason'). Ideology doesn't need believers - it operates through practices, rituals, and structures even when subjects are cynically aware.

questions.question: In Blanchot's literary theory, the 'space of literature' is characterized by:
options: The dissolution of the author-subject in the impersonal, anonymous murmur of language itself - writing as the experience of dispossession
questions.explanation: Blanchot: writing is not expression of a subject but the subject's effacement. Literature opens onto an 'outside' - the impersonal space of language where the writer encounters their own disappearance. Foucault and Derrida drew heavily on this.

questions.question: What is 'metalepsis' in narratology?
options: A figure of speech using metals as metaphors
A transgression of the boundary between narrative levels - when a narrator enters their own story or a character addresses the author
A type of narrative time distortion
The study of narratives about narratives
questions.explanation: Metalepsis (Genette) is the violation of narrative boundaries: a character 'stepping out' of the story, an author entering their fictional world, or a frame narrative collapsing into its embedded story. It destabilizes the ontological status of fiction.

questions.question: Nancy's concept of 'being singular plural' means:
options: Existence is always already shared - there is no singular being that precedes its relation to others; being is constitutively co-being
questions.explanation: Jean-Luc Nancy: being is not first individual then social. Being IS singular plural - every singularity is constituted through its exposure to others. There is no 'I' before 'we.' This ontological co-existence is prior to any community or society.
